question: Explain the Strategy pattern and give a production example.
shortAnswer: Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface. The client chooses which strategy to use at runtime without modifying its code. In SGN3, validation logic uses this — different enrollment types (regular, scholarship, transfer) have different validation rules, each in its own class implementing a common Validacao interface.
detailedAnswer:
Strategy pattern:
- Define a family of algorithms (strategies)
- Encapsulate each in its own class implementing a common interface
- Client delegates to the strategy without knowing which one

Structure:
```
Context → Strategy interface → ConcreteStrategyA, ConcreteStrategyB
```

Benefits:
- Open/Closed: add new strategies without modifying context
- Testable: test each strategy in isolation
- Eliminates conditional logic (if/else chains)

When to use:
- Multiple variants of the same algorithm
- Need to switch behavior at runtime
- if/else chain based on type is growing

When NOT to use:
- Only 2 simple, stable variants (if/else is fine)
- Strategies share significant state (might need different pattern)
- Adds indirection without real benefit (YAGNI)
keyPoints: Open/Closed — add strategies without modifying context
Know when NOT to use (YAGNI for 2 simple cases)
redFlags: Cannot give a real example beyond textbook sorting
Not knowing the relationship to OCP

followUps.question: How do you select the right strategy at runtime?
followUps.hint: Factory method, DI container qualifier, Map<Type, Strategy>, or configuration.

followUps.question: What is the difference between Strategy and Template Method?
followUps.hint: Strategy: composition (HAS-A behavior). Template Method: inheritance (IS-A with hooks).

realExperience.title: SGN3 validation strategies per enrollment type
realExperience.description: Different enrollment types (regular, transfer, scholarship) have different validation rules. Each implemented as a separate Validacao class. The Business layer selects the correct validator based on the enrollment type — no giant if/else.

question: When would you use the Builder pattern?
shortAnswer: When constructing an object with many optional parameters, or when construction is complex (multi-step, validation needed). Builder makes construction readable (named methods instead of positional args) and can enforce invariants at build time. I use it for DTOs with 10+ fields and for test data factories.
detailedAnswer:
Builder pattern solves:
1. Telescoping constructor problem (10+ params, hard to read)
2. Complex construction logic (validation, defaults, derived values)
3. Immutable objects with many fields

When to use:
- Object has many optional parameters
- Construction has steps (set A, derive B from A, validate)
- You want readable, self-documenting construction
- Immutable objects (build once, don't modify)

When NOT to use:
- Simple objects with 2-3 fields (just use constructor)
- Mutable objects where setters are fine
- Adds boilerplate without real benefit

In Java: Lombok @Builder reduces boilerplate. Without Lombok, static inner class pattern.

In tests: Builder pattern for test data factories — readable, reusable, with sensible defaults:
```java
Turma turma = TurmaBuilder.umaTurma()
    .comSituacao(ATIVA)
    .comModalidade(TECNICO)
    .build();
```
keyPoints: Can enforce invariants at build() time
Lombok @Builder reduces boilerplate in production code
redFlags: Cannot explain when NOT to use it
Never used it in tests (missed biggest use case)

followUps.question: How does Builder relate to immutability?
followUps.hint: Builder collects params, build() creates an immutable instance. Only way to construct complex immutable objects.

followUps.question: When would a record be better than Builder?
followUps.hint: When all fields are required and the object is simple. Records are constructors + immutability built-in.

realExperience.title: SGN3 test data builders
realExperience.description: Used Builder pattern extensively for test data factories (TurmaBuilder, MatriculaBuilder). Each builder provides sensible defaults — tests only specify what they care about. Makes tests readable and maintainable.

question: How do you decide which design pattern to apply? What is your process?
shortAnswer: I don't start from patterns — I start from the problem. Patterns are names for recurring solutions. My process: identify the force (what varies? what is coupled? what changes?), recall if a known pattern addresses that force, then evaluate if the pattern's trade-offs are acceptable for the context. Most of the time, simple composition with clear interfaces is enough.
detailedAnswer:
Pattern selection process:

1. **Identify the design force** (the tension):
   - What varies? → Strategy, Template Method
   - What is created differently? → Factory, Builder
   - What needs to notify others? → Observer, Event
   - What should have one instance? → Singleton (via DI container)
   - What needs decoration? → Decorator

2. **Evaluate trade-offs**:
   - Does it add justified complexity?
   - Will the team understand it?
   - Is it solving a real problem or hypothetical one?

3. **Consider simpler alternatives**:
   - Sometimes a clear interface + DI is all you need
   - Not everything needs a named pattern
   - YAGNI: don't pattern for hypothetical future

In practice, 90% of SGN3 design decisions use three things:
- Interfaces + CDI injection (covers Strategy, Factory, Observer)
- Composition over inheritance
- Clear layer separation (WebBean → Service → Business → Repository)

Named patterns are vocabulary for communication, not a checklist to apply.
keyPoints: Start from the PROBLEM, not from the pattern
Identify the design force (what varies? what changes?)
Evaluate trade-offs for the specific context
Simple interface + DI covers most pattern use cases
Patterns are vocabulary, not a checklist
redFlags: "I always apply X pattern" without context
Cannot explain WHEN a pattern is inappropriate
Over-engineering simple code with unnecessary patterns

followUps.question: Give an example of a pattern you decided NOT to apply.
followUps.hint: Shows restraint and judgment. YAGNI in practice.

followUps.question: What pattern do you see most abused?
followUps.hint: Singleton (global state), or over-applied Strategy for 2 simple cases.

realExperience.title: SGN3 pragmatic pattern usage
realExperience.description: Most design in SGN3 is solved by: interfaces + CDI injection + layer separation. Named patterns appear naturally (Repository, Strategy for validators, Observer via CDI events) but are not forced. The architecture IS the pattern.
